title: Radiographic Image Quality Parameters Review
narrationScript: Four words decide every film you will ever sign. Density, contrast, definition, and sensitivity. This lesson rebuilds each one, and more important, the way they trade against each other, because at Level Two you are no longer adjusting one knob. You are balancing a system. Density is the platform. It is a logarithm of light transmission, so a density of one passes ten percent of the illuminator light, a density of two passes one percent, and a density of three passes one tenth of one percent. Codes window density for single film viewing, typically about one point eight to four for x ray techniques and two to four for gamma. The floor exists because film contrast collapses at low density. The ceiling exists because ordinary illuminators cannot push enough light through very dark film for the eye to discriminate. Density is measured, never estimated, with a densitometer checked against a calibrated step tablet, and it is read through the area of interest, including through the body of the image quality indicator. Contrast is the size of the signal, and it is a product of two parts. Subject contrast is made in the part. It is the ratio of intensities transmitted by different thicknesses, and energy is its master control. Drop the energy and photoelectric absorption punishes every small thickness change, so contrast rises while latitude, the range of thickness you can capture in one density window, shrinks. Raise the energy and the response flattens. More latitude, less contrast. Scatter is the thief in this story. Fog adds density everywhere without adding information, diluting the very differences you exposed for, which is why masks, collimators, filters, and lead screens are really contrast tools. Film contrast is made in the film and the darkroom. It is the gradient of the characteristic curve at your working density, and it climbs as density rises up the straight line region, which is one reason experienced interpreters aim for the upper half of the window. It also depends on film class and on development held to time and temperature. Definition is how cleanly the edges are drawn. Geometric unsharpness from the last lesson is one term. Motion is another, and it smears edges exactly like camera shake. Film graininess grows with film speed and with energy, and it eats fine detail. Screens matter too. Lead foil screens sharpen the picture by filtering scatter while they intensify, but only when contact with the film is tight. Fluorescent screens buy speed and pay for it in blur. Total unsharpness is ruled by the largest single term, so find the biggest offender and fix that one first. Sensitivity is the outcome, the smallest detail the radiograph can demonstrate, and it is proven, not assumed. The image quality indicator is the proof. A hole type plaque is normally two percent of the part thickness, with holes of one, two, and four times the plaque thickness. The familiar two dash two T quality level means the plaque is two percent of the thickness and the hole you must see is twice the plaque thickness. On a one inch part, that is a twenty thousandths of an inch plaque with a forty thousandths hole. Wire sets work the same way with graded wire diameters laid across the weld. Place the indicator on the source side whenever you can reach it, because that is the worst case geometry. If it must go on the film side, a lead letter F sits beside it so nobody misreads the setup. On welds with reinforcement, the plaque sits on a shim that matches the reinforcement, so it reports the truth about the full thickness being inspected. And be precise about what a visible essential hole proves. It proves that the whole chain, energy, exposure, geometry, screens, processing, and density, delivered the required sensitivity on this film. It does not promise that every flaw of that size will show. A tight crack at the wrong angle can still hide. Put it all together and technique design becomes deliberate trading. Raising energy buys latitude and speed and spends contrast. Raising exposure buys density and gradient until it overshoots the window. Lengthening distance buys sharpness and spends time. Finer film buys detail and spends speed. When you optimize, move one lever at a time, watch all four parameters, and let the indicator on the film, read at proper density, tell you when the system is in balance. That is the whole craft in one sentence.
imagePrompt: A film interpreter in a darkened reading room leans over a glowing high-intensity illuminator, examining a weld radiograph through a handheld magnifier with a densitometer and a reference step wedge within reach.

sections.heading: Four Words That Decide Every Film
sections.body:
Every radiograph you accept or reject is judged through four parameters.

- **Density** - overall film darkness: how much exposure reached and developed in the emulsion. Density is the platform; outside its working window nothing else can be evaluated.
- **Contrast** - the density *difference* between a feature and its background. Contrast is the size of the signal.
- **Definition** - the sharpness of edges. Definition is how cleanly the signal is drawn.
- **Sensitivity** - the smallest detail the radiograph can demonstrate, proven on the film by an IQI. Sensitivity is the *outcome*: it improves when contrast and definition improve, standing on adequate density.

A 2% quality level - demonstrating a thickness change of about two percent of the part - is the common benchmark. The parameters are not independent: nearly every technique lever moves two of them at once, often in opposite directions. Level II work is choosing those trades deliberately, which is why this review ends with an interaction map instead of a list of definitions.

sections.heading: Density: The Platform
sections.body:
Film density is a logarithm of light transmission: **D = log₁₀(I₀/I_t)**. Density 1 passes 10% of the illuminator's light, density 2 passes 1%, density 3 passes 0.1%, density 4 passes 0.01%. The log scale is why a film that looks only a little darker may hold ten times the blocking power.

Codes window density for single-film viewing - typically about **1.8 to 4.0 for X-ray** and **2.0 to 4.0 for gamma** techniques, measured through the area of interest including through the IQI body. The bounds exist for cause. Below the floor, the film sits low on its characteristic curve where the **gradient - film contrast - is poor**, so real thickness changes produce feeble density differences. Above the ceiling, ordinary illuminators cannot push enough light through for the eye to discriminate.

Density is **measured, never estimated**: a densitometer checked against a calibrated step tablet, with readings taken through the area of interest and recorded. Codes also limit how much density may vary across that area relative to the reading through the IQI - a wedge-shaped part or sloppy masking can push the edges out of tolerance even when the center reads beautifully. When a shot misses the window, fix the exposure, not the illuminator: density is the parameter that last lesson's exposure arithmetic corrects directly.

sections.caption: The characteristic curve: film contrast (gradient) climbs with density up the straight-line region, which is why codes window density and interpreters favor the upper half of the window.

sections.heading: Contrast: Subject Times Film
sections.body:
Radiographic contrast is the product of two contrasts.

**Subject contrast** is made in the part: the ratio of radiation intensities transmitted through its different thicknesses. Energy is the master control. At low energy, photoelectric absorption punishes thickness differences, so a small step produces a large transmission ratio - high subject contrast, narrow **latitude** (the thickness range that fits inside the density window). At high energy, Compton flattens the response: more latitude, less contrast. Scatter is the thief - fog from Compton events adds equal density everywhere, diluting the very differences you exposed for. Masking, collimation, filters, and lead screens are contrast-recovery tools.

**Film contrast** is made in the film and the darkroom: the **gradient** of the characteristic curve at the density you achieved. It rises with density up the straight-line region - one reason interpreters favor the upper half of the window - and it depends on film class (finer-grain, slower films generally deliver more gradient) and on development held to time-temperature spec.

The two multiply. A perfect film cannot rescue a flat subject; brilliant subject contrast can be squandered on a thin, underdeveloped film. When a radiograph looks gray and lifeless, diagnose in order: energy too high? scatter unmanaged? density too low on the curve? development weak? Each has a different fix, and separating subject from film contrast points you to the right one.

sections.heading: Definition: The Edge Story
sections.body:
Definition is edge sharpness - how abruptly density changes at a boundary. Its enemies add together:

- **Geometric unsharpness**: Ug = F·t/d from the last lesson - small source, long distance, film tight to the part.
- **Motion**: vibration of part, source, or film during exposure smears edges exactly like camera shake. Cassette clamps and guide-tube stands exist for this reason.
- **Film graininess**: developed silver grains cluster; faster films and higher energies produce coarser, more mottled structure that eats fine detail. Film system classes grade this - finer grain means slower but sharper.
- **Screen unsharpness and contact**: lead foil screens (commonly 0.005-0.010 in. front and back) actually *help* definition - electron intensification plus scatter filtering - when contact is intimate. Fluorescent screens buy large speed factors and pay for them in light spread: visibly softer images. Poor screen-film contact lets emitted electrons and light diffuse sideways, a localized blur that can mask real indications.

Total unsharpness is dominated by the **largest single term**, so fix the biggest offender first: there is no point halving Ug while shooting fluorescent screens on a vibrating line. In the field, the usual order of suspicion is contact and motion, then geometry, then film and screen choice.

sections.caption: Simulated weld radiograph: hole-type plaque on a shim beside the weld with its 1T, 2T, and 4T holes and lead ID number, a wire IQI laid across the weld, and a location marker.

sections.title: Decoding 2-2T
sections.body: The first character is the **plaque thickness**: 2% of the part thickness. The T term names the **essential hole** in multiples of plaque thickness: 1T, 2T, or 4T diameter. **2-2T on a 1.0-in. part means a 0.020-in.-thick plaque whose 0.040-in. (2T) hole must be visible** at the required density. Demonstrating it proves roughly 2% thickness sensitivity for this technique on this film.

sections.heading: IQIs: Proving It on the Film
sections.body:
The image quality indicator turns good technique from an opinion into evidence. Two families:

- **Hole-type plaques**: a plaque whose thickness is keyed to part thickness (the 2% level is standard), pierced with 1T, 2T, and 4T holes and carrying a lead identification number.
- **Wire-type sets**: graded wire diameters in a sleeve laid **across the weld**; the required (essential) wire must be visible against the weld image.

Rules a Level II must own. Place the IQI on the **source side** whenever access allows - that is the worst-case geometry. Film-side placement is the exception and must be flagged with a **lead letter F** beside the IQI so no reader mistakes the setup. Under a plaque on a weld with reinforcement, add a **shim** of radiographically similar material matching the reinforcement, so the plaque sits at the full representative thickness - otherwise it reads at parent-metal density and proves nothing about the weld. Select the IQI size from the governing code's tables using the thickness that includes allowed reinforcement.

Be precise about what visibility proves: the **entire chain** - energy, exposure, geometry, screens, processing, density - delivered the required sensitivity on this radiograph. It does **not** guarantee that every flaw of that size is visible; a tight crack at the wrong angle can hide on a film whose 2T hole shines. The IQI qualifies the technique. Judgment still reads the film.

sections.caption: One lever, three effects - every technique change moves more than one parameter, and the craft is choosing the trade deliberately.
rows: Film gradient up at the higher density

figures.title: The characteristic curve: where film contrast lives

interactive.title: Prove the Sensitivity
interactive.intro: The procedure requires 2-2T hole-type sensitivity with a source-side IQI. On this radiograph, click the one feature whose visibility accepts the image.

regions.label: 4T hole
regions.feedback: The 4T hole is the easiest to see. A visible 4T alone only demonstrates 2-4T quality - easier than the required 2-2T.

regions.label: 2T hole
regions.feedback: Correct. With a plaque 2% of part thickness, the visible 2T hole is the essential hole that demonstrates 2-2T sensitivity.

regions.label: 1T hole
regions.feedback: Seeing 1T would exceed the requirement, but acceptance is judged on the essential 2T hole - the 1T may legitimately be invisible.

regions.label: Plaque ID number
regions.feedback: The lead identification number tells you which plaque was used. It proves identity, not sensitivity.

regions.label: Wire IQI
regions.feedback: Wires demonstrate sensitivity in wire-IQI systems, but this procedure specified hole-type 2-2T, so the essential 2T hole governs acceptance.

checkQuestions.question: A region of a radiograph reads density 3.0. How much of the illuminator's light passes through it?
options: 3 percent
1 percent
0.1 percent
30 percent
checkQuestions.explanation: Density is log₁₀(I₀/I_t), so D = 3.0 means a transmission ratio of 1000:1 - one tenth of one percent of the light gets through.

checkQuestions.question: Why do gamma-ray techniques typically carry a minimum density requirement of about 2.0?
options: Below it the film gradient is too low, so contrast is inadequate
Below it the film cannot be fixed properly
Lower densities fade during storage
Illuminators are too bright for densities under 2.0
checkQuestions.explanation: Low on the characteristic curve the gradient is shallow, so a real thickness change produces only a feeble density difference. The floor keeps the film on the steep, high-contrast part of its curve.

checkQuestions.question: Raising the kilovoltage while holding film density constant generally does what?
options: Raises subject contrast and narrows latitude
Lowers subject contrast and widens latitude
Improves definition by shrinking the focal spot
Has no effect other than shortening exposure
checkQuestions.explanation: Higher-energy photons flatten the transmission differences between thicknesses: less subject contrast, but a wider thickness range fits inside the density window.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of changes all act to improve definition?
options: Smaller source, film tight to the part, longer source-to-object distance, finer-grain film
Higher kV, faster film, fluorescent screens
More mA, more time, higher density
Larger focal spot moved closer to the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Definition is controlled by Ug = F·t/d (smaller F, smaller t, larger d) plus film graininess - finer-grain film classes draw sharper edges.

checkQuestions.question: For a 0.50-in.-thick weld, the 2-2T quality level calls for which plaque-type IQI?
options: A 0.020-in. plaque with a 0.040-in. hole visible
A 0.010-in. plaque with a 0.020-in. hole visible
A 0.010-in. plaque with a 0.010-in. hole visible
A 0.040-in. plaque with a 0.080-in. hole visible
checkQuestions.explanation: The plaque is 2% of 0.50 in. = 0.010 in. thick, and the essential 2T hole is twice the plaque thickness: 0.020 in. diameter.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course, FQ 1.5 (radiographic image quality parameters)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2: density windows, IQI selection and placement (general reference)
Standards such as ASTM E1025 (hole-type) and ASTM E747 (wire-type) IQI designs (general reference)
